ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Burmese rice salad (htamin thoke). FOODON_00002809 (rice dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Burmese htamin thoke. A000E (rice-based dishes) is the closest defensible parent node.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Burmese rice salad or htamin thoke.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Pyay Htamin Thoke is approximately 300 g, equivalent to one plate as typically served at a Burmese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked white rice and mixed salad component estimation based on FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Pyay Htamin Thoke (Burmese mixed rice salad) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from the primary components: cooked white rice (~65% by weight), fried onions, dried shrimp, sesame oil, fish sauce, chickpea powder, lime juice, and herbs. Cooked white rice values sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA; oil and seasoning contributions from USDA SR Legacy. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white rice (boiled) as analogous base; Atkinson FS et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pyay Htamin Thoke. The GI is estimated from boiled white rice (GI ~64, medium), which constitutes approximately 65% of the dish by weight. The addition of sesame oil, fish sauce, dried shrimp, chickpea powder, and lime juice is expected to modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain rice due to fat, protein, and acid content; however, rice remains the dominant glycemic driver. GI of 64 is used as a conservative analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving = (64 × 27.5 × 300) / (100 × 100) = 52.8 g per 300 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and dried shrimp (~420 mg sodium per 100 g; ~1260 mg per standard 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced fish sauce, as a single serving can contribute over 50% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 64) driven by white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source, with approximately 82.5 g of carbohydrate per 300 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size carefully, as the medium GI and high carbohydrate load per serving can cause a significant postprandial glucose rise; substituting brown rice or reducing serving size to 150–200 g may help manage glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium from fish sauce and dried shrimp, with moderate fat from sesame oil; saturated fat is relatively low at 0.7 g per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content is the primary concern; the dish's sesame oil contributes beneficial unsaturated fats, but overall sodium intake should be managed by limiting portion size or requesting lower-sodium preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pyay Htamin Thoke (ပြည်ထမင်းသုတ်) is a traditional Burmese rice salad dish originating from Myanmar. The name translates roughly as 'Pyay mixed rice' or 'Burmese-style rice salad', with 'thoke' (သုတ်) meaning a hand-tossed salad in Burmese culinary terminology. The dish is prepared by combining warm or room-temperature cooked white rice with a dressing of fish sauce, lime juice, and sesame oil, then tossing it together with fried shallots or onions, dried shrimp, roasted chickpea powder (besan), fresh chilli, coriander, and spring onion. The result is a fragrant, savoury, and slightly tangy rice salad with a complex layering of umami from the fish sauce and dried shrimp, nuttiness from the sesame oil and chickpea powder, and brightness from the lime and herbs. The texture contrasts soft rice grains with the crunch of fried onions and the chewiness of dried shrimp. Pyay Htamin Thoke is commonly eaten as a light meal or snack in Myanmar and is available at Burmese restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish (~168 kcal per 100 g) with a medium glycemic index (~64) due to its white rice base. The dish is high in sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) owing to fish sauce and dried shrimp, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It provides moderate protein (~4.8 g per 100 g) from dried shrimp and chickpea powder, and beneficial unsaturated fats from sesame oil. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to fish-derived ingredients.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Pyay Htamin Thoke exists in any primary approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA as a composite dish). Nutrient values are estimated from component-level data using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy. GI is estimated by analogy with boiled white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-level estimates; no direct composite dish entry was found in approved primary sources. Values should be validated against laboratory analysis or a future approved composite entry if one becomes available. 2. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish in the component sources used. 3. Vitamin B6 (vitamin_b6_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this dish composition. 4. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources; dried shrimp may contribute a small amount but a defensible value cannot be assigned. 5. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 6. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources. 7. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources. 8. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources. 9. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy component data. 10. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on boiled white rice (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021); the actual GI of the composite dish may be lower due to fat, protein, and acid contributions from dressing ingredients. 11. The cuisine_tags in the input included 'Noodle' and 'Lor Mee', which appear to be field survey platform tagging errors; this dish is a rice salad, not a noodle dish. These tags have been disregarded. 12. Burmese cuisine does not fall under the Singaporean multilingual naming requirement (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi); the mandatory language for this cuisine is the native Burmese script, which is captured in aliases_by_language under burmese_script and in the flat aliases list.
